issue_title: Poor label quality: "Subscribe to our mailing list"
actual_result: The label 'Subscribe to our mailing list' describes an action or outcome rather than the data the user is expected to enter. A user scanning the form cannot immediately tell that an email address is required.; The label does not identify the type or format of input expected (i.e. an email address), which reduces sufficiency — it cannot stand alone as a clear field descriptor without the placeholder 'Email' providing implicit clarification.; Relying on the placeholder 'Email' to communicate the required input type is insufficient under WCAG 2.1, as placeholders disappear on input and are not a reliable label substitute.
expected_result: Form field labels should be descriptive and meaningful
recommendation: Use a label that clearly identifies the expected input, such as 'Email address'. If the subscription context is important, consider pairing a descriptive heading or fieldset legend (e.g. 'Subscribe to our mailing list') with a properly labelled field (e.g. 'Email address') so both the purpose and the required data are communicated clearly.

issue_title: Required field not clearly indicated: "Subscribe to our mailing list"
actual_result: The effective label 'Subscribe to our mailing list' does not indicate that the field is required (e.g. no asterisk, no '(required)' text).; No instructions (aria-describedby) are present to communicate the required nature of the field.; The placeholder value 'Email' also does not indicate the field is required. Placeholders are not a reliable substitute for labels or instructions and disappear on input.; While the 'required' attribute is programmatically set, not all assistive technologies surface this consistently, and sighted users have no visual cue that the field is mandatory before attempting submission.
expected_result: Required field status should be communicated visually and programmatically
recommendation: Update the visible label to explicitly communicate that the field is required, for example: 'Subscribe to our mailing list (Email, required)' or add a clearly associated secondary label/description such as 'Email address * (required)'. If a form-level convention is used (e.g. '* denotes a required field'), ensure that notice appears before the form fields in the DOM and is visually prominent. This satisfies WCAG 3.3.2 by ensuring users are informed of the requirement before attempting to submit.

actual_result: The filename strongly suggests this is a promotional poster (likely for a film, play, or event titled 'Shakuntala') intended for Facebook sharing. Posters are inherently informational — they typically contain titles, dates, credits, or other meaningful content that conveys purpose and identity. A poster is almost never purely decorative.
expected_result: Image marked as decorative (alt="") should truly be decorative
recommendation: Add descriptive alt text that conveys the key information visible in the poster, e.g., alt="Shakuntala [event/film] promotional poster" or a more detailed description including any text, imagery, or context shown in the poster.

actual_result: The filename explicitly identifies this as a banner for 'VisionAid', which is almost certainly an organization, campaign, or program name. Banners are promotional or informational by nature and typically contain branding, messaging, or calls to action. The specific naming convention (including dimensions 800x600) suggests it is a purposefully created content asset, not a decorative graphic.
expected_result: Image marked as decorative (alt="") should truly be decorative
recommendation: Add descriptive alt text that identifies the organization and conveys the banner's message, e.g., alt="VisionAid [brief description of banner content or campaign message]". If the banner contains text, that text should be reflected in the alt attribute.

issue_title: Actionable image alt issue: "Map of India"
actual_result: Alt text describes the visual appearance of the image ('Map of India') rather than the action. However, the button has button_text 'INDIA' and button_aria_label 'Select India', so the image alt is redundant and could be empty alt='' since the button already has a sufficient accessible name.
expected_result: Images in links/buttons should describe the action/destination, not appearance
recommendation: Since the button already has visible text 'INDIA' and aria-label 'Select India', set alt="" to avoid redundancy. The button's accessible name alone is sufficient.

actual_result: Missing role="img" — screen readers will not announce this SVG as an image; Missing <title> element — no programmatic label is available for assistive technologies; Missing aria-labelledby — no association between the SVG and a descriptive label; Missing aria-label — no fallback accessible name is provided; SVG has no accessible name by any supported method (title, aria-label, aria-labelledby)
expected_result: SVGs should have role="img" and an accessible name via title + aria-labelledby
recommendation: If this SVG is informative, apply the full WCAG-compliant pattern: add role="img" to the <svg> element, insert a <title id="svg-title-[unique-id]"> with a concise, meaningful description of the image's purpose, and link it via aria-labelledby="svg-title-[unique-id]". Optionally add a <desc> element for a longer description. Example: <svg role="img" aria-labelledby="svg-title-1"><title id="svg-title-1">A bar chart showing monthly sales growth from January to June 2024</title></svg>. If the SVG is purely decorative, add aria-hidden="true" to remove it from the accessibility tree entirely.

actual_result: Icon is the sole content of its control (link or button) but aria-hidden=true hides it from screen readers; Control has no accessible name — completely invisible to assistive technology
expected_result: Icon fonts should be properly labeled or hidden from assistive technology
recommendation: Remove aria-hidden=true and add aria-label to the parent control (e.g., <button aria-label="Search">) or add visually hidden text inside the control. The icon itself can then remain aria-hidden=true as a decorative supplement to that label.

actual_result: aria-hidden is not set, so the screen reader will attempt to announce this icon; No aria-label and no visible or sibling text — screen reader will read the raw class name or nothing meaningful; Icon font pseudo-content (e.g., Unicode glyph) may be announced verbatim, creating a confusing experience
expected_result: Icon fonts should be properly labeled or hidden from assistive technology
recommendation: Determine the icon's role in context. If it is purely decorative (e.g., a visual affordance alongside a labelled dropdown), add aria-hidden="true". If it conveys state or meaning on its own (e.g., expand/collapse indicator), either add aria-label to the icon element or, preferably, add aria-expanded and an accessible name to the parent control and then set aria-hidden="true" on the icon.
